data.description:

Key Insights

  • The considerable ownership by retail investors in Ceres indicates that they collectively have a greater say in management and business strategy
  • 51% of the business is held by the top 10 shareholders
  • Insiders own 23% of Ceres

To get a sense of who is truly in control of Ceres Inc. (TSE:3696), it is important to understand the ownership structure of the business. With 38% stake, retail investors possess the maximum shares in the company. Put another way, the group faces the maximum upside potential (or downside risk).

While the holdings of retail investors took a hit after last week’s 11% price drop, institutions with their 34% holdings also suffered.

Let's delve deeper into each type of owner of Ceres, beginning with the chart below.

Check out our latest analysis for Ceres

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Ceres?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

As you can see, institutional investors have a fair amount of stake in Ceres. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. If multiple institutions change their view on a stock at the same time, you could see the share price drop fast. It's therefore worth looking at Ceres' earnings history below. Of course, the future is what really matters.

Ceres is not owned by hedge funds. With a 17% stake, CEO Satoshi Takagi is the largest shareholder. For context, the second largest shareholder holds about 8.8% of the shares outstanding, followed by an ownership of 7.2% by the third-largest shareholder.

On further inspection, we found that more than half the company's shares are owned by the top 10 shareholders, suggesting that the interests of the larger shareholders are balanced out to an extent by the smaller ones.

While it makes sense to study institutional ownership data for a company, it also makes sense to study analyst sentiments to know which way the wind is blowing. Our information suggests that there isn't any analyst coverage of the stock, so it is probably little known.

Insider Ownership Of Ceres

The definition of company insiders can be subjective and does vary between jurisdictions. Our data reflects individual insiders, capturing board members at the very least. The company management answer to the board and the latter should represent the interests of shareholders. Notably, sometimes top-level managers are on the board themselves.

I generally consider insider ownership to be a good thing. However, on some occasions it makes it more difficult for other shareholders to hold the board accountable for decisions.

It seems insiders own a significant proportion of Ceres Inc.. Insiders have a JP¥4.3b stake in this JP¥19b business. It is great to see insiders so invested in the business. It might be worth checking if those insiders have been buying recently.

General Public Ownership

The general public-- including retail investors -- own 38% stake in the company, and hence can't easily be ignored. While this size of ownership may not be enough to sway a policy decision in their favour, they can still make a collective impact on company policies.

Public Company Ownership

Public companies currently own 4.3% of Ceres stock. We can't be certain but it is quite possible this is a strategic stake. The businesses may be similar, or work together.

Next Steps:

It's always worth thinking about the different groups who own shares in a company. But to understand Ceres better, we need to consider many other factors. For instance, we've identified 2 warning signs for Ceres that you should be aware of.

data.title: These 4 Measures Indicate That Fuji SeikiLtd (TSE:6400) Is Using Debt Reasonably Well
data.description:
Legendary fund manager Li Lu (who Charlie Munger backed) once said, 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. Importantly, Fuji Seiki Co.,Ltd. (TSE:6400) does carry debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. If things get really bad, the lenders can take control of the business. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

How Much Debt Does Fuji SeikiLtd Carry?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 Fuji SeikiLtd had debt of JP¥3.73b, up from JP¥3.33b in one year. However, it does have JP¥1.81b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about JP¥1.92b.

How Healthy Is Fuji SeikiLtd's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Fuji SeikiLtd had liabilities of JP¥3.78b due within 12 months, and liabilities of JP¥2.26b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of JP¥1.81b as well as receivables valued at JP¥1.76b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by JP¥2.48b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of JP¥2.35b, we think shareholders really should watch Fuji SeikiLtd's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

Check out our latest analysis for Fuji SeikiLtd

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). The advantage of this approach is that we take into account both the absolute quantum of debt (with net debt to EBITDA) and the actual interest expenses associated with that debt (with its interest cover ratio).

We'd say that Fuji SeikiLtd's moderate net debt to EBITDA ratio ( being 1.7), indicates prudence when it comes to debt. And its strong interest cover of 12.2 times, makes us even more comfortable. Also good is that Fuji SeikiLtd grew its EBIT at 18% over the last year, further increasing its ability to manage debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But you can't view debt in total isolation; since Fuji SeikiLtd will need earnings to service that debt. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So it's worth checking how much of that EBIT is backed by free cash flow. During the last three years, Fuji SeikiLtd generated free cash flow amounting to a very robust 90% of its EBIT, more than we'd expect. That puts it in a very strong position to pay down debt.

Our View

The good news is that Fuji SeikiLtd's demonstrated ability to cover its interest expense with its EBIT delights us like a fluffy puppy does a toddler. But the stark truth is that we are concerned by its level of total liabilities. Looking at all the aforementioned factors together, it strikes us that Fuji SeikiLtd can handle its debt fairly comfortably. On the plus side, this leverage can boost shareholder returns, but the potential downside is more risk of loss, so it's worth monitoring the balance sheet. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. For example, we've discovered 3 warning signs for Fuji SeikiLtd (1 makes us a bit uncomfortable!) that you should be aware of before investing here.

Of course, if you're the type of investor who prefers buying stocks without the burden of debt, then don't hesitate to discover our exclusive list of net cash growth stocks, today.

data.title: Does YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372) Create Value For Shareholders?
data.description:
One of the best investments we can make is in our own knowledge and skill set. With that in mind, this article will work through how we can use Return On Equity (ROE) to better understand a business. We'll use ROE to examine YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372), by way of a worked example.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. In simpler terms, it measures the profitability of a company in relation to shareholder's equity.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Do You Calculate Return On Equity?

Return on equity can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for YMIRLINKInc is:

15% = JP¥451m ÷ JP¥2.9b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the profit over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every ¥1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of ¥0.15.

View our latest analysis for YMIRLINKInc

Does YMIRLINKInc Have A Good Return On Equity?

One simple way to determine if a company has a good return on equity is to compare it to the average for its industry. Importantly, this is far from a perfect measure, because companies differ significantly within the same industry classification. If you look at the image below, you can see YMIRLINKInc has a similar ROE to the average in the Software industry classification (14%).

That's neither particularly good, nor bad. While at least the ROE is not lower than the industry, its still worth checking what role the company's debt plays as high debt levels relative to equity may also make the ROE appear high. If true, then it is more an indication of risk than the potential.

The Importance Of Debt To Return On Equity

Most companies need money -- from somewhere -- to grow their profits. That cash can come from retained earnings, issuing new shares (equity), or debt. In the first and second cases, the ROE will reflect this use of cash for investment in the business. In the latter case, the use of debt will improve the returns, but will not change the equity. That will make the ROE look better than if no debt was used.

YMIRLINKInc's Debt And Its 15% ROE

One positive for shareholders is that YMIRLINKInc does not have any net debt! Its respectable ROE suggests it is a business worth watching, but it's even better the company achieved this without leverage. At the end of the day, when a company has zero debt, it is in a better position to take future growth opportunities.

Conclusion

Return on equity is useful for comparing the quality of different businesses. A company that can achieve a high return on equity without debt could be considered a high quality business. If two companies have around the same level of debt to equity, and one has a higher ROE, I'd generally prefer the one with higher ROE.

Having said that, while ROE is a useful indicator of business quality, you'll have to look at a whole range of factors to determine the right price to buy a stock. The rate at which profits are likely to grow, relative to the expectations of profit growth reflected in the current price, must be considered, too. data.title: Returns On Capital Are A Standout For Timee (TSE:215A)
data.description:
If you're not sure where to start when looking for the next multi-bagger, there are a few key trends you should keep an eye out for. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. Speaking of which, we noticed some great changes in Timee's (TSE:215A) returns on capital, so let's have a look.

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Timee:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.44 = JP¥6.7b ÷ (JP¥34b - JP¥18b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to October 2025).

So, Timee has an ROCE of 44%. That's a fantastic return and not only that, it outpaces the average of 20% earned by companies in a similar industry.

See our latest analysis for Timee

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Timee compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free analyst report for Timee .

The Trend Of ROCE

The trends we've noticed at Timee are quite reassuring. Over the last two years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 44%. The amount of capital employed has increased too, by 113%. The increasing returns on a growing amount of capital is common amongst multi-baggers and that's why we're impressed.

Another thing to note, Timee has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 55%. This can bring about some risks because the company is basically operating with a rather large reliance on its suppliers or other sorts of short-term creditors. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

The Bottom Line On Timee's ROCE

To sum it up, Timee has proven it can reinvest in the business and generate higher returns on that capital employed, which is terrific. Given the stock has declined 35% in the last year, this could be a good investment if the valuation and other metrics are also appealing. That being the case, research into the company's current valuation metrics and future prospects seems fitting.

Timee does have some risks, we noticed 2 warning signs (and 1 which doesn't sit too well with us) we think you should know about.

data.title: Yubico (OM:YUBICO) Net Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) has wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of 548.3 million SEK and basic EPS of 0.04 SEK, setting the tone for a year where trailing 12 month revenue came in at 2.2 billion SEK and basic EPS was 1.49 SEK. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between 499.1 million SEK and 623.1 million SEK over the past year, with basic EPS ranging from 0.04 SEK to 0.74 SEK. Trailing 12 month EPS and net income have tracked alongside 12 month revenue in the low single digit billions of SEK. For investors, the key question now is how the current net margin compression shapes the quality and durability of those earnings.

See our full analysis for Yubico.

With the scorecard set, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing stories about Yubico, where growth expectations and margin pressures pull in different directions.

See what the community is saying about Yubico

Net margin slips to 5.8%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Yubico earned net income of SEK128.1 million on SEK2.2b of revenue, which works out to a 5.8% net margin compared with 16% a year earlier.
  • Bears focus on that margin compression as a sign that hardware focused economics are under pressure, yet
    • they also assume in their own narrative that margins could reach 13.7% in about three years, which would be higher than the current 5.8% even though recent quarters include Q4 FY 2025 net income of just SEK3.7 million on SEK548.3 million of revenue, a relatively thin quarter.
    • this gap between today’s 5.8% trailing margin and the implied 13.7% margin view shows that even cautious analysts are anchoring their concerns to what happens from here, not to the latest reported profitability alone.
If you are weighing whether this margin drop is a temporary blip or a longer term issue, it helps to see how skeptics frame the downside case in detail: 🐻 Yubico Bear Case

EPS swings against bullish hopes

  • Quarterly basic EPS for FY 2025 moved from SEK0.59 in Q1 to SEK0.74 in Q3, then down to SEK0.04 in Q4, while trailing 12 month EPS fell from SEK4.32 in Q4 FY 2024 to SEK1.49 by Q4 FY 2025.
  • Bulls talk about earnings growing roughly 38.4% per year over the next few years and, in their scenario, profits rising from SEK254.5 million today to SEK719.2 million by around 2028, yet
    • the current trailing EPS of SEK1.49 and Q4 EPS of SEK0.04 show that, for now, earnings sit a long way below those bullish outcomes and have moved down from the SEK4.32 trailing EPS level seen a year earlier.
    • this contrast means anyone leaning on the bullish view is effectively saying the subscription shift and enterprise roll outs will need to lift earnings from today’s reset base, not from the higher profitability implied by last year’s trailing numbers.
If you want to see how optimistic investors connect these EPS swings to their long term story, the full bullish case lays it out: 🐂 Yubico Bull Case

High P/E and big DCF gap

  • At a share price of SEK54.08, Yubico trades on a trailing P/E of 36.5x compared with a Swedish software industry average of 20.4x, while also sitting well below the stated DCF fair value of SEK134.33 and an allowed analyst price target of SEK85.00.
  • Consensus style views point to forecast revenue growth of about 12.7% per year and earnings growth around 38.4% per year, and that mix creates a tension where
    • the elevated 36.5x P/E versus the 20.4x industry level reflects investors paying more for each krona of trailing earnings, even though trailing margins have moved from 16% to 5.8%.
    • at the same time, the gap between SEK54.08 and SEK134.33 on the DCF fair value, as well as the SEK85.00 analyst target, shows how much of the upside case depends on those growth forecasts materialising over the next few years.

data.title: Telus (TSX:T) Margin Improvement Tests Bullish Profitability Narratives

Net margin at 5.8% puts more weight on profitability quality

  • On a trailing basis TELUS reports a 5.8% net profit margin versus 4.6% a year earlier, and CA$1.2b of net income on CA$20.4b of revenue, so a bigger share of each dollar of sales is currently turning into profit.
  • What stands out against the bullish view is that, while bulls are looking for margins to reach 8.7%, the last twelve months are still anchored around 5.8%,

Skeptics warn that if interest coverage and cash generation do not improve, the mix of a premium P/E, high dividend yield and elevated leverage could keep pressure on how the market values TELUS over time. 🐻 TELUS Bear Case

Compared with the broader European Food group at 17.3x and peers at 26.8x, Viscofan’s 16.4x multiple looks restrained rather than stretched, even though it sits just above the internally estimated fair ratio of 16.2x. data.title: Is Heineken (ENXTAM:HEIA) Pricing Look Interesting After Recent Share Price Strength
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Heineken at around €78 a share still offers value, the key question is how its current price compares with what the business might reasonably be worth.
  • The stock has recently been firm, with returns of 6.0% over the last 7 days, 14.4% over the last 30 days, 13.2% year to date, and 0.8% over the last year. However, the 3 year and 5 year returns of negative 12.9% and negative 1.0% show a much flatter longer term picture.
  • Recent news around Heineken has focused on its position as a major global brewer and how it is managing brand strength and operations in different markets. This helps frame how investors think about its long term prospects. These themes often influence how the market prices the shares, particularly when sentiment shifts between focusing on shorter term share price moves and longer term fundamentals.
  • Right now, Heineken scores 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks, giving it a valuation score of 3. Next we will look at what different valuation methods indicate about that number, before finishing with a way of thinking about value that can give you a fuller picture than any single model.

Find out why Heineken's 0.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Heineken Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future euros are worth in today’s money.

For Heineken, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €2.57b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2028, with Simply Wall St then extrapolating free cash flow out to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow for 2026 is €2.66b and for 2035 is €3.39b, with intermediate years gradually stepping up between those figures.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €150.96 per share. Compared with a recent share price around €78, this output suggests the stock is 48.3% undervalued according to this DCF model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Heineken is undervalued by 48.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 234 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Heineken.

Approach 2: Heineken Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Heineken, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they feel those earnings are.

Heineken currently trades on a P/E of 23.05x. That sits above the Beverage industry average of 17.72x, but slightly below the peer group average of 24.82x. On their own, these comparisons show where the market is pricing Heineken relative to its sector and closest competitors, but they do not fully reflect the company’s specific growth outlook, profitability or risk profile.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio tackles that by estimating what a company’s P/E might be given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and identified risks. For Heineken, this Fair Ratio is 21.36x, which is below the current 23.05x P/E. On this basis, the shares screens as somewhat expensive relative to what the Fair Ratio model suggests would be a more neutral level.

Result: OVERVALUED

On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives to set your own view on Heineken, compare it with others, and see in real time how each view changes when new earnings or news arrive. For example, one Heineken Narrative might lean closer to a fair value around €64.62 with more cautious assumptions, while another leans toward €110.19 with more optimistic expectations. By comparing each Narrative’s Fair Value with today’s share price, you get a clear, easy to use framework for assessing whether the stock looks expensive, cheap or roughly in line with your own story.

For Heineken however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Heineken Narratives:

Implied discount to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 8.3% below the narrative fair value

  • Assumes gradual improvement in profit margins and steady earnings growth, supported by cost savings and digital efficiency programs.
  • Flags currency swings, reliance on emerging markets and pressure in mature European markets as the main things that could upset this story.

🐻 Heineken Bear Case

  • Analysts' consensus view expects margins to move higher even if revenue softens, which creates an interesting tension:
    • Forecasts in the inputs point to revenue declining about 1.4% per year over the next three years, yet margins are assumed to rise from 6.3% to 7.4%, so the story leans on Raute keeping more profit from each euro of sales.

Approach 1: Qiagen Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It focuses on cash the business can generate for shareholders rather than just current earnings multiples.

For Qiagen, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $466.9 million. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected Free Cash Flow of $593 million in 2026 and $663 million by 2029, with further projections out to 2035 all expressed in $. Simply Wall St discounts each of these projected cash flows back to today to arrive at an estimated intrinsic value per share.

On this basis, the DCF model points to a fair value of about $66.15 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $50.85, this implies Qiagen trades at roughly a 23.1% discount. On this cash flow based view, the stock currently screens as undervalued.

Approach 2: Qiagen Price vs Earnings

The P/E ratio is a useful way to value a profitable business because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when they expect slower growth or perceive higher risk.
